item.title: “Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by…
item.description:
“Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving. It is possible, monks, that the great ocean might dry up, be exhausted, and cease to exist. But I say that beings shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, wandering and roaming on, will not have made an end of suffering. It is possible, monks, that Sineru, the king of mountains, might be destroyed and cease to exist. But I say that beings shrouded by ignorance … will not have made an end of suffering. It is possible, monks, that the great earth might be destroyed and cease to exist. But I say that beings shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, wandering and roaming on, will not have made an end of suffering!”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.99
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, those ascetics or brahmins who do not know as it actually is the gratification as gratification, the peril as peril, the escape as escape…
item.description:
“Monks, those ascetics or brahmins who do not know as it actually is the gratification as gratification, the peril as peril, the escape as escape in the case of forms — they will certainly not understand forms as they actually are, nor can they lead others to a similar position, namely, understanding forms as they actually are — such a thing is not possible. Monks, those ascetics or brahmins who know as it actually is the gratification as gratification, the peril as peril, the escape as escape in the case of forms — they will certainly understand forms as they actually are, and they can lead others to a similar position, namely, understanding forms as they actually are — such a thing is possible.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 13 – Mahādukkhakkhandhasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “O brahmin, strive to cut the stream, Abandon sensual pleasures. Knowing the destruction of formations, You will be the one who knows the Unmade.” “By…
item.description:
“O brahmin, strive to cut the stream,
Abandon sensual pleasures.
Knowing the destruction of formations,
You will be the one who knows the Unmade.”

“By always dwelling in two Dhammas,
One reaches the further shore.
That brahmin, wise,
Cuts off all fetters.”

“One who has no near shore, no far shore,
No both shores,
Free from suffering, unbound —
Him I call a brahmin.”

“Meditating, free from dust,
Duty done, without defilements,
Having attained the highest goal —
Him I call a brahmin.”

From: Dhammapada 383-423
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Venerable sir, ‘stain’ is called a stain. What term is synonymous with what is called ‘stain’?” “Venerable sir, evil, unwholesome states, the realm of sensual…
item.description:
“Venerable sir, ‘stain’ is called a stain. What term is synonymous with what is called ‘stain’?”
“Venerable sir, evil, unwholesome states, the realm of sensual desire, are synonymous with what is called ‘stain.’”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 5 – Anaṅgaṇasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, sensual thought is blind, causes blindness, causes lack of knowledge, leads to the destruction of wisdom, partakes of distress, does not lead to Nibbāna.…
item.description:
“Monks, sensual thought is blind, causes blindness, causes lack of knowledge, leads to the destruction of wisdom, partakes of distress, does not lead to Nibbāna. Monks, ill-will thought is blind … harmful thought is blind, causes blindness, causes lack of knowledge, leads to the destruction of wisdom, partakes of distress, does not lead to Nibbāna.

Three thoughts of wholesome kind
One should frequently reflect upon —
Three unwholesome thoughts should be abandoned, turned away from.
That one surely restrains
The conduct of thoughts.

As rain settles the dust,
So by calming thoughts with the mind,
One here and now attains
The peaceful path of peace.”

From: Itivuttaka 87
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “‘The ocean, the ocean,’ monks, an untaught ordinary person says. That, monks, is not the ocean in the noble one’s discipline. That is just a…
item.description:
“‘The ocean, the ocean,’ monks, an untaught ordinary person says. That, monks, is not the ocean in the noble one’s discipline. That is just a great mass of water, a great flood. Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. This, monks, is called the ocean in the noble one’s discipline. Here, this world with its devas, Māras, Brahmās, with its ascetics and brahmins, with its devas and humans, for the most part is submerged in it, tangled like a ball of thread, covered like a tangled skein, become like a tangled grass rope, unable to pass beyond the plane of misery, the bad destination, the lower realm, saṃsāra.

Having destroyed ignorance, greed, and hatred,
This ocean with its many dangers —
With its sharks, crocodiles,
Whirlpools, and terrifying waves —
Is very hard to cross.

Having crossed the bond, escaped death,
Free from attachments,
Having made an end of suffering,
There is no further becoming.
He has destroyed birth and death,
The incomparable one —
I say that such a one
Has blinded Death.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.229
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as great desire. For one with…
item.description:
“I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as great desire. For one with great desire, monks, unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 1.62
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Good, venerable sir, if the Blessed One would teach me the Dhamma in brief. Having heard the Dhamma from the Blessed One, I will dwell…
item.description:
“Good, venerable sir, if the Blessed One would teach me the Dhamma in brief. Having heard the Dhamma from the Blessed One, I will dwell alone, secluded, heedful, ardent, and resolute.” - “Koṭṭhita, whatever is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. And what, Koṭṭhita, is suffering? The eye is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. Forms are suffering — there you should abandon your desire. Eye-consciousness is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. Eye-contact is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. Whatever feeling arises dependent on eye-contact — pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant — that feeling is suffering. There you should abandon your desire.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.163
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “‘Come, monk, guard the sense faculties. When you see a form with the eye, do not grasp the general feature, do not grasp the particular…
item.description:
“‘Come, monk, guard the sense faculties. When you see a form with the eye, do not grasp the general feature, do not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the eye faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise — restrain that cause, guard the eye, practice restraint of the eye. When you hear a sound with the ear … smell an odor with the nose … taste a flavor with the tongue … feel a touch with the body … cognize a phenomenon with the mind, do not grasp the general feature, do not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the mind faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise — restrain that cause, guard the mind, practice restraint of the mind.’”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 107 – Gaṇakamoggallānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “If he feels a pleasant feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent.’ He understands: ‘I am not attached to it.’ He understands: ‘I do not delight…
item.description:
“If he feels a pleasant feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent.’ He understands: ‘I am not attached to it.’ He understands: ‘I do not delight in it.’ If he feels a painful feeling … If he feels a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent.’ He understands: ‘I am not attached to it.’ He understands: ‘I do not delight in it.’ If he feels a pleasant feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. If he feels a painful feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. If he feels a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. When he feels a feeling ending with the body, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling ending with the body.’ When he feels a feeling ending with life, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling ending with life.’ He understands: ‘With the breakup of the body, after death, everything that is felt, in which he no longer delights, will become cool right here.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 36.7
📖 Read this sutta
